slides.title: Don't Repeat Yourself 🚫
slides.content:
In programming, there's a golden rule called **DRY** (Don't Repeat Yourself).

If you find yourself copying and pasting the exact same 10 lines of code in 5 different places, you're doing it wrong! What happens if you need to fix a bug in those 10 lines? You have to fix it 5 times.

slides.title: The Solution: Functions 📦
slides.content: A **Function** is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You write the code *once* inside the function, and then you "call" that function by its name whenever you need it to run.

slides.title: Creating a Function
slides.content:
To create a function, you need three things before the name: a **Return Type** (what it sends back), a **Name**, and **Parentheses `()`**.

`void` means this function does NOT return any value back.

quizData.question: What does the keyword `void` mean when placed in front of a function name?

options.text: It means the function destroys data.
It means the function does not return any data/value back to where it was called.
It means the function is broken.

quizData.explanation: Void literally means 'empty'. It tells C: 'Run this code, but don't expect it to hand any calculated answer back to me.'

slides.title: Order Matters ⚠️
slides.content:
In C, the compiler reads from top to bottom. If `main()` tries to call `sayHello()`, but `sayHello()` is written *below* main, the compiler will crash! It doesn't know what `sayHello()` is yet.

Always put your functions **ABOVE** `main()` (or use Function Prototypes, which we'll learn later).

quizData.question: Why will the C compiler throw an error if you call a function that is defined at the very bottom of the file (below main)?

options.text: Because main() must always be the absolute last line of code.
Because C compilers read top-to-bottom and haven't seen the function definition yet.
Because main() is selfish and doesn't share memory.

quizData.explanation: C is a 1-pass compiler (mostly). If it encounters a name it hasn't read yet, it panics.
